content.text: What it is

Non-functional requirements (NFRs) for agentic AI features define the quality attributes that the system must satisfy independently of what it does. While functional requirements specify features and behaviors, NFRs specify how well the system must perform, how reliably it must operate, how securely it must handle data, and how much it can cost to run. For generative AI systems, the standard set of NFR categories — performance, reliability, security, scalability — must be extended with AI-specific categories: output quality, hallucination rates, cost per inference, token throughput, and behavior consistency across model versions. Without explicit AI NFRs, production systems routinely fail in ways that are invisible to standard software reliability metrics: they can be highly available yet consistently wrong, fast but dangerously confident in hallucinated information.
Why it matters

Traditional software NFRs were designed for deterministic systems where the same input always produces the same output. Generative AI systems violate this assumption in fundamental ways: the same prompt can produce different outputs across calls, model upgrades can silently change behavior, and costs scale with token usage rather than compute cycles. These differences mean that teams managing AI feature quality need a richer set of measurable NFR targets. Without them, engineering teams have no objective criteria for deciding whether a feature is ready for production, whether a model upgrade is safe to ship, or whether cost growth is acceptable at scale.
Benefits
Objective production readiness criteria: teams can make fact-based go/no-go decisions instead of subjective judgments about whether the AI feature is good enough.
Model upgrade safety: NFRs for behavior consistency detect regressions introduced by new model versions before they reach users.
Cost predictability: token budget and cost-per-request NFRs prevent runaway spend as usage scales, enabling financial forecasting alongside feature planning.
Architectural guidance: latency and throughput NFRs drive decisions about caching, streaming, model tier selection, and batch processing strategies.
Stakeholder confidence: explicit, measurable NFRs let product and legal stakeholders verify that quality and safety requirements are actually met, not just asserted.
Trade-offs and limitations
Some NFRs are genuinely hard to measure: hallucination rate requires human evaluation or a reliable LLM-as-judge, both of which are expensive and imperfect.
NFR targets can conflict: a latency NFR of under 500ms may be incompatible with a quality NFR requiring deep reasoning over long contexts.
Model provider dependencies: some NFRs (token throughput, TTFT) are bounded by the model provider's infrastructure and cannot be met by application-level engineering alone.
NFR drift: as models change, previously met NFR targets may require renegotiation — the threshold that was right for one model version may be wrong for the next.
How it works

NFRs for agentic AI systems span six categories. Performance NFRs cover time-to-first-token (TTFT), token generation throughput, and end-to-end response latency, typically expressed as p50/p95/p99 percentiles. Quality NFRs cover evaluation pass rate, hallucination rate measured on a held-out test set, and human review sampling targets. Cost NFRs specify cost per 1,000 requests, monthly spend caps, and token budget limits per call. Reliability NFRs define availability targets (e.g., 99.9% uptime), maximum consecutive failure rate, and fallback behavior when the primary model is unavailable. Security NFRs require prompt injection defense, PII detection and filtering, output sanitization, and comprehensive audit logging. Observability NFRs mandate that every LLM call is traced, token usage is reported, and quality drift triggers alerts.

Each NFR must be expressed in measurable terms with a specific numeric target (not vague language like 'fast' or 'reliable'), a measurement method, and a measurement cadence. Targets should differentiate between production and staging environments and should specify the percentile at which latency targets apply. In regulated environments, NFRs should also include an escalation owner — a named person responsible for responding when an NFR threshold is breached.
Architecture

attrs.alt: NFR categories for generative AI systems: performance, reliability, security, cost, quality/accuracy, and observability arranged around a central GenAI NFRs hub
attrs.title: Non-Functional Requirement Categories for GenAI

content.text: The six NFR categories for GenAI systems form an interconnected set: performance targets drive the observability instrumentation needed to measure them, cost constraints influence the model tier chosen for performance, and reliability targets determine the fallback strategies needed when performance NFRs cannot be met. Quality and security NFRs cross-cut all categories and must be verified independently of the other dimensions.
When to use it

Define AI-specific NFRs at the start of every production AI feature — not during the PoC, but before committing to build. The NFR elicitation session should involve engineers (who set technically feasible targets), product managers (who understand acceptable user experience), finance (who set cost budgets), and security (who specify compliance requirements). The session typically takes 90 minutes for a new feature and 30 minutes for an upgrade to an existing feature. Revisit NFRs when: the model version changes, traffic grows by more than 5x, a new market or regulatory jurisdiction is added, or a production incident reveals that an existing NFR was insufficient.
How to implement
Start from an NFR template that enumerates all six categories. For each category, fill in the measurable target, measurement method, measurement cadence, and escalation owner.

Benchmark baseline metrics against the model and infrastructure you plan to use. Never set latency or throughput targets without running actual load tests first — provider-published benchmarks rarely match production conditions.

Identify which NFRs conflict and document explicit trade-off decisions. For example, if latency and quality conflict, document that the team chose a smaller, faster model and accepted a 3% lower evaluation pass rate.
Build monitoring dashboards for each measurable NFR. Wire alerts to PagerDuty or equivalent for NFR breaches that require immediate action.
Add NFR validation to the CI pipeline using automated checks for performance and cost NFRs. For quality NFRs, run evaluation suite pass-rate checks on every PR that touches the system prompt.
Schedule quarterly NFR reviews to update targets as the feature matures, traffic grows, and model capabilities evolve.
Examples
An NFR specification in YAML format suitable for version control alongside the behavior spec:

# feature: document-summarizer
# version: 2.1.0

nfrs:
  performance:
    time_to_first_token:
      target_p50_ms: 400
      target_p95_ms: 800
      target_p99_ms: 1500
      measurement: "Prometheus histogram from api-gateway span"
    throughput_per_second:
      target: 40  # tokens/second streaming output
      measurement: "Client-side streaming speed measurement"
    end_to_end_latency_ms:
      target_p95: 8000   # includes network + model time for 2k token output
      measurement: "Datadog APM trace from request to stream close"

  quality:
    eval_pass_rate:
      target: 0.92
      suite: "tests/evals/doc-summarizer/"
      cadence: "every PR + nightly on production sample"
    hallucination_rate:
      target_max: 0.03   # < 3% of summaries contain unverifiable claims
      measurement: "Weekly LLM-as-judge sample of 200 production outputs"
      judge_model: "claude-opus-4-5"
    user_satisfaction:
      target_thumbs_up_rate: 0.80
      measurement: "In-product feedback widget, 7-day rolling average"

  cost:
    cost_per_1k_requests_usd:
      target_max: 4.50
      model: "claude-haiku-4-5"   # primary; falls back to claude-sonnet-4-5
      measurement: "Anthropic usage dashboard + internal billing aggregation"
    monthly_spend_cap_usd: 12000
    token_budget_per_call:
      input_tokens_max: 8000
      output_tokens_max: 1024

  reliability:
    availability:
      target: 0.999    # 99.9% uptime
      measurement: "Pingdom synthetic monitor every 60s"
    consecutive_failure_max: 3
    fallback_behavior: "Return cached summary if available; else show error with retry CTA"
    circuit_breaker:
      failure_threshold: 5
      window_seconds: 30
      cooldown_seconds: 60

  security:
    prompt_injection_defense:
      target: "all known injection patterns blocked"
      measurement: "Red team evaluation quarterly"
    pii_filter:
      required: true
      enforcement: "pre-processing strip before model call"
    audit_log:
      required: true
      fields: [user_id, session_id, input_hash, output_hash, model_version, timestamp]
      retention_days: 365

  observability:
    trace_coverage: 1.0    # 100% of LLM calls traced
    token_usage_reporting: true
    quality_drift_alert:
      eval_pass_rate_drop_threshold: 0.05
      action: "page on-call engineer"
    prompt_version_in_logs: true
A Python script that loads the NFR spec and runs an automated check against live telemetry:

import yaml
import time
import anthropic
from pathlib import Path

def check_performance_nfrs(spec_path: str, model: str) -> dict:
    """Run a latency + cost check against the NFR spec."""
    spec = yaml.safe_load(Path(spec_path).read_text())
    perf = spec["nfrs"]["performance"]
    cost_spec = spec["nfrs"]["cost"]
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    results = []
    for _ in range(10):  # sample 10 calls
        start = time.time()
        first_token_time = None
        tokens_used = {"input": 0, "output": 0}

        with client.messages.stream(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=cost_spec["token_budget_per_call"]["output_tokens_max"],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize: " + ("long document text..." * 100)}]
        ) as stream:
            for event in stream:
                if first_token_time is None and hasattr(event, "delta"):
                    first_token_time = time.time()
            message = stream.get_final_message()
            tokens_used["input"] = message.usage.input_tokens
            tokens_used["output"] = message.usage.output_tokens

        ttft_ms = (first_token_time - start) * 1000 if first_token_time else 99999
        total_ms = (time.time() - start) * 1000
        results.append({"ttft_ms": ttft_ms, "total_ms": total_ms, "tokens": tokens_used})

    ttfts = sorted(r["ttft_ms"] for r in results)
    p95_ttft = ttfts[int(len(ttfts) * 0.95)]
    target_p95 = perf["time_to_first_token"]["target_p95_ms"]

    return {
        "p95_ttft_ms": p95_ttft,
        "target_p95_ms": target_p95,
        "nfr_met": p95_ttft <= target_p95,
        "total_tokens": sum(r["tokens"]["input"] + r["tokens"]["output"] for r in results),
    }
Real-world example

A legal technology company launched an AI contract review assistant with no documented NFRs. After three months of production traffic, the feature was consuming 0,000 per month in token costs — six times the projected budget — because the average input context grew from 2,000 tokens (the PoC assumption) to 12,000 tokens (actual contract length). At the same time, the operations team was receiving no alerts about quality drift: the evaluation pass rate had dropped from 94% to 81% after a model upgrade, but no one noticed until lawyers started flagging missed clauses.

The remediation required three sprints. The first sprint established formal NFRs: a cost cap of 5,000/month (requiring input truncation and chunked processing), a quality NFR of ≥90% eval pass rate (triggering an immediate model rollback), and a p95 latency NFR of 12 seconds (which surfaced that streaming had never been implemented). The second sprint built the monitoring to measure all six NFR categories. The third sprint brought all NFRs into compliance. The total remediation cost — engineering time plus the three months of excess spend — was higher than the original specification and monitoring investment would have been by a factor of eight.
Common mistakes
Setting NFR targets based on PoC token counts rather than production usage patterns — real users always send longer, more complex inputs than PoC scenarios.
Defining latency NFRs without specifying whether they apply to streaming first-token latency or full response latency — these differ by 5–10x and require different optimization strategies.
Treating quality NFRs as optional because they are harder to measure than latency, which is precisely backwards from a risk standpoint.
Omitting model upgrade impact from the NFR change control process, so model version bumps bypass the NFR review that would catch behavioral regressions.
Specifying NFRs in absolute terms (under 1 second) rather than percentile terms (p95 under 1 second), which makes them unmeasurable in a statistically meaningful way.
Best practices
Express every NFR with a specific numeric target, a measurement method, and a measurement cadence — all three are required for an NFR to be enforceable.
Distinguish between hard NFRs (feature cannot ship without meeting these) and soft NFRs (monitored but not a launch blocker) to avoid gate creep.
Benchmark NFR targets before committing to them: run load tests, check model provider SLAs, and verify that targets are achievable at the planned infrastructure scale.
Include fallback behavior as an NFR: specify what the system should do when the primary model is unavailable, slow, or returning low-quality outputs.
Version the NFR document alongside the behavior specification and the system prompt, and require NFR review for any change that might affect the quality, cost, or latency of model calls.
Performance and scalability

At scale, NFRs become harder to satisfy simultaneously. The cost NFR imposes pressure to use smaller, cheaper models; the quality NFR imposes pressure to use larger, more capable models. This tension must be resolved explicitly at design time. Common patterns include: model tiering (use a cheap model for simple queries, a capable model only when needed), prompt caching (reduce cost and latency by caching frequent system prompts), context compression (reduce input token counts to stay within budget), and evaluation-gated model selection (run both models in shadow mode and route based on evaluation scores).

As request volume grows beyond ~100 requests per second, NFRs around rate limiting and burst handling become critical. The NFR spec should define a burst tolerance NFR (e.g., the system can handle 3x average traffic for up to 60 seconds without latency degradation), which drives decisions about request queuing, batch processing, and provider quota management.

Security and reliability

Security NFRs for AI systems should specify both preventive controls (input filtering, output sanitization, prompt injection defense) and detective controls (audit logging, anomaly detection, adversarial red-team evaluation schedules). The audit log NFR is particularly important for regulated industries: it provides the evidentiary trail required to demonstrate that the AI system operated within defined parameters during a specific time window.

Reliability NFRs must account for AI-specific failure modes beyond normal service failures. These include: the model returning semantically valid but factually incorrect outputs (quality failure, not availability failure), the model becoming consistently slow during high-demand periods from shared infrastructure, and model behavior changing after a provider-side update without prior notification. Each of these failure modes requires a different monitoring and response strategy that traditional reliability engineering does not address.
NFR Monitoring Implementation

Monitoring all six NFR categories in a unified way requires purpose-built instrumentation. The following example shows how to implement structured logging that captures all dimensions of an LLM call in a single event, making it easy to build dashboards and alerts for every NFR category without separate instrumentation for each.

import time
import json
import hashlib
import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class LLMCallMetrics:
    feature_id: str
    model: str
    prompt_version: str
    user_id: str
    session_id: str
    input_hash: str
    output_hash: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    ttft_ms: float
    total_latency_ms: float
    cost_usd: float
    error: Optional[str]
    timestamp: str

# Anthropic token pricing (claude-haiku-4-5 as example)
TOKEN_COSTS = {
    "claude-haiku-4-5": {"input": 0.00025 / 1000, "output": 0.00125 / 1000},
    "claude-sonnet-4-5": {"input": 0.003 / 1000, "output": 0.015 / 1000},
}

def traced_llm_call(
    client: anthropic.Anthropic,
    feature_id: str,
    prompt_version: str,
    user_id: str,
    session_id: str,
    system_prompt: str,
    user_message: str,
    model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5",
    max_tokens: int = 1024,
) -> tuple[str, LLMCallMetrics]:
    """
    Wrapper around the Anthropic API that captures all NFR-relevant metrics.
    Emits a structured log event compatible with Datadog, Splunk, or CloudWatch.
    """
    import datetime

    start = time.perf_counter()
    first_token_time = None
    output_text = ""
    error_msg = None

    input_hash = hashlib.sha256(user_message.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

    try:
        with client.messages.stream(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
            system=system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
        ) as stream:
            for chunk in stream.text_stream:
                if first_token_time is None:
                    first_token_time = time.perf_counter()
                output_text += chunk
            final = stream.get_final_message()
            input_tokens = final.usage.input_tokens
            output_tokens = final.usage.output_tokens
    except Exception as e:
        error_msg = str(e)
        input_tokens = output_tokens = 0
        first_token_time = first_token_time or time.perf_counter()

    end = time.perf_counter()
    pricing = TOKEN_COSTS.get(model, {"input": 0, "output": 0})
    cost = (input_tokens * pricing["input"]) + (output_tokens * pricing["output"])

    metrics = LLMCallMetrics(
        feature_id=feature_id,
        model=model,
        prompt_version=prompt_version,
        user_id=user_id,
        session_id=session_id,
        input_hash=input_hash,
        output_hash=hashlib.sha256(output_text.encode()).hexdigest()[:16],
        input_tokens=input_tokens,
        output_tokens=output_tokens,
        ttft_ms=(first_token_time - start) * 1000,
        total_latency_ms=(end - start) * 1000,
        cost_usd=round(cost, 6),
        error=error_msg,
        timestamp=datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
    )
    # Emit to structured log — route to Datadog / Splunk / CloudWatch
    print(json.dumps({"event": "llm_call", **asdict(metrics)}))
    return output_text, metrics

This single instrumentation point provides data for all six NFR categories: the latency fields feed performance dashboards, token counts and cost feed cost monitoring, the error field feeds reliability alerts, input_hash and output_hash feed audit logging for security, prompt_version enables quality drift detection when correlated with evaluation scores, and the full structured event feeds observability pipelines. Teams that instrument every LLM call this way from day one spend significantly less time hunting for data when an NFR breach occurs in production.
Key takeaways
Generative AI systems need six NFR categories, not two: performance, quality, cost, reliability, security, and observability.
Every NFR must have a specific numeric target, a measurement method, and a cadence — vague requirements are not enforceable.
Quality NFRs (hallucination rate, eval pass rate) are often the most important and the most neglected; prioritize them alongside latency and availability.
Cost NFRs based on PoC token assumptions routinely underestimate production spend by 3–10x; always baseline against real usage data before setting targets.
Include model version changes in the NFR change control process, as model upgrades can silently change quality, cost, and latency simultaneously.
NFR conflicts (latency vs. quality, cost vs. capability) must be documented and resolved explicitly as architectural decisions, not left as implicit trade-offs.
Co-locate the NFR document with the behavior specification in version control so that they evolve together and are reviewed together on every change.
